title: Debt & Credit
description: Understand financial leverage, credit utilization, arbitrage, debt strategies, and using credit as a professional tool.

blocks.text: The Concept of Financial Leverage Leverage is the strategic use of borrowed money to increase the potential return on an investment. For example, using a mortgage to buy a rental property allows you to control a large asset with a small amount of your own cash, potentially amplifying your gains if the property value rises. However, leverage is a double-edged sword; while it amplifies gains, it also amplifies losses and increases risk. Intermediate users learn to use leverage only for productive, income-generating assets rather than consumer items. Action: Research a "buy-to-let" scenario. Calculate the ROI if you bought a property with 100% cash versus a 25% down payment and a mortgage.

lessons.title: Credit Utilization Ratio and Your Score

sections.title: Credit Utilization Ratio and Your Score

blocks.text: Credit Utilization Ratio and Your Score Your credit score is heavily influenced by your "Credit Utilization Ratio," which is the amount of credit you are using compared to your total available limit. High utilization—such as using 90% of your limit—signals high risk to banks and can significantly lower your score. Intermediate users aim to keep this ratio below 30%. Lowering your utilization is the fastest way to "boost" your credit score without needing an increase in income. Action: Divide your total current credit card balances by your total credit limits. If the result is over 30%, create a plan to pay it down.

Arbitrage: Borrowing to Earn Arbitrage is the practice of taking advantage of interest rate differences between two markets. In finance, this could involve taking a low-interest loan (e.g., 3%) and investing that money in a guaranteed or high-probability asset that pays a higher rate (e.g., 6%). The difference between the two rates is your net profit. This is an advanced-intermediate move that requires high discipline and should never be attempted with high-risk "destructive" debt. Action: Look at the interest rate of a low-cost personal loan and compare it to the current yield of a low-risk government bond or a reliable dividend fund.

The Debt-to-Income (DTI) Ceiling Banks use the Debt-to-Income (DTI) ratio—the percentage of gross monthly income that goes toward paying debts—to decide if you are "over-leveraged". Most lenders have a strict ceiling, typically between 35% and 43%. Intermediate mastery involves managing your DTI proactively to maintain "Financial Agility". Keeping your debt payments well below the ceiling ensures that when a great investment opportunity arises, you still have the "borrowing capacity" to act quickly. Action: Calculate your DTI: (Total Monthly Debt Payments / Gross Monthly Income) x 100. If it's over 35%, prioritize debt reduction.

There are two primary strategies for paying off debt: the Snowball and the Avalanche. The Snowball method focuses on paying off the smallest balances first to gain psychological momentum, while the Avalanche method focuses on paying off the highest interest rates first to save the most money mathematically.

Intermediate users choose the method that fits their personality, knowing that consistency is more important than perfect math. By sticking to a structured plan, they ensure that every unit of capital is moving them closer to a debt-free life.
Action: List your debts by both "Balance Size" and "Interest Rate" to see which strategy (psychological vs. mathematical) appeals to you more.

lessons.title: Good Debt vs. Bad Debt: The ROI Test

sections.title: Good Debt vs. Bad Debt: The ROI Test

blocks.text: Intermediate users distinguish debt based on its impact on their net worth. "Bad Debt" is used for consumer items that lose value (like clothes or electronics), while "Good Debt" is used for assets that grow or produce income (like mortgages or business loans).

The ultimate test for any debt is the ROI (Return on Investment). If the interest rate on the debt is significantly lower than the expected return on the asset being purchased, it can be considered a productive tool for building wealth.
Action: Audit your current debts. Label each one as "Productive" (Good) or "Destructive" (Bad).

Co-signing a loan for someone else is one of the riskiest financial moves you can make. When you co-sign, you are 100% legally responsible for the debt, and it appears on your credit report, potentially lowering your score and borrowing capacity.

Intermediate users avoid co-signing because it is a "High Risk, Zero Reward" scenario. If the primary borrower defaults, your financial future is at stake. It is better to give a cash gift you can afford to lose rather than linking your legal credit to someone else's behavior.
Action: If asked to co-sign, offer a one-time cash gift you can afford to lose instead of linking your legal credit to the other person.

Refinancing involves taking out a new loan at a lower interest rate to pay off an existing, more expensive debt. This is particularly effective for large, long-term debts like mortgages, where even a 1% drop in interest can save you thousands of riyals over the life of the loan.

Intermediate users proactively monitor market rates. By refinancing when rates drop, they effectively "buy back" their own cash flow, reducing monthly payments and accelerating their journey to full ownership.
Action: Check the current interest rate for your largest debt and compare it to the rates currently being offered to new customers.

lessons.title: Using Credit as a Professional Tool

sections.title: Using Credit as a Professional Tool

blocks.text: For intermediate users, credit is not a way to spend money they don't have, but a professional tool to manage cash flow. They use credit cards to earn rewards, cashback, and travel points, while benefiting from advanced consumer protections.

The key to this strategy is paying the balance in full every single month. By never carrying a balance, they enjoy all the perks of credit systems for free, while simultaneously building a top-tier credit score that grants them access to low-interest capital in the future.
Action: Ensure all your credit cards are set to "Auto-Pay" for the full statement balance to avoid accidental interest charges.

lessons.title: Credit Utilization Strategy for Limit Increases

sections.title: Credit Utilization Strategy for Limit Increases

blocks.text: A sophisticated way to lower your credit utilization ratio is to request a limit increase on your existing cards without increasing your spending. This immediately makes your used credit a smaller percentage of your total available credit, boosting your score.

Intermediate users time these requests during periods of high income or after several months of perfect payment history. This proactive management of "borrowing capacity" ensures they always have the highest possible credit score when they actually need to apply for a major loan.
Action: Contact your credit card provider and request a credit limit increase on your oldest card to lower your utilization ratio.
